one_line_takeaway: STEAD leverages the parallel, position-independent denoising of diffusion language models to embed repetition-coded messages at 'robust positions', achieving provably secure steganography that, unlike autoregressive-model methods, survives bounded token substitution/insertion/deletion while offering ~6x higher capacity than the prior robust baseline PSARS.
key_finding: On the DREAM 7B DLM at top-p=1.0, STEAD achieves an effective embedding capacity of 84.08 bits per 1000 tokens versus only 13.81 for the ARM-based robust baseline PSARS (QWEN2), roughly a 6x improvement, while also being faster (0.9859 vs 1.6583 s/bit encoding). Steganalysis detection error rates hover near 50% (49.00%-50.67%), i.e. steganalyzers do no better than random guessing, confirming statistical security. For robustness, under a word-level semantic synonym substitution attack at rate 0.1, non-robust ARM-based PSS methods (Discop, SparSamp) are rendered almost entirely ineffective while STEAD sustains an extraction correctness rate above 80%. The ablation shows the baseline DLM without RPE/ECC/NSE is only as robust as ARM methods, confirming that STEAD's gains come from its embedding/extraction design, not merely from switching to a DLM.
core_contribution: STEAD is a provably secure AND robust linguistic steganography framework built on discrete/masked diffusion language models (DLMs) rather than autoregressive models (ARMs). The paper's own novelty is three-fold: (1) it identifies that within a single DLM denoising step multiple masked positions are unmasked in parallel and independently, and exploits these as a batch of 'robust positions' where the same message can be embedded redundantly without the cross-token error propagation that plagues ARM-based provably secure steganography (PSS); (2) a message-driven pseudo-random-number (PRN) sampling scheme with fixed embedding capacity that only replaces the sampling function (not the model's predicted distribution), preserving provable security, combined with repetition error-correcting codes (ECC) at robust positions ('robust position embedding with ECC'); and (3) a neighborhood-search extraction (NSE) strategy plus pseudo-random error correction at extraction time to recover from token insertions/deletions that cause positional misalignment. Note: Meteor, Discop, SparSamp (ARM-based PSS) and PSARS are prior/concurrent work the paper compares against, NOT STEAD's own contribution.
threat_model: A probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) man-in-the-middle adversary in a highly regulated/censored environment (e.g. a social-media operator or email provider under government policy) who intercepts stegotext on a public channel, alters it, and republishes it. The adversary has two capabilities: (a) detection/steganalysis (distinguishing stegotext from model-sampled covertext), against which STEAD must be provably secure; and (b) bounded tampering, formalized as a tampering function f_t in a feasible set F_{alpha,beta,gamma} that limits substitutions, insertions, and deletions to at most alpha*L, beta*L, gamma*L of the length-L text. The bound reflects that unlimited alteration is infeasible in practice (business/regulatory cost). STEAD is proven Fα,β,γ-robust when 2(alpha+beta+gamma) < min_s(N_s/L) and beta+gamma < mu/L, where N_s is the number of robust positions per step and mu the neighborhood window.
limitations: By the authors' own framing: (1) robustness holds only under bounded tampering F_{alpha,beta,gamma} with explicit inequality conditions (2(alpha+beta+gamma) < min_s N_s/L and beta+gamma < mu/L); beyond these bounds guarantees fail. (2) The repetition-code choice sacrifices capacity for the strongest robustness, and messages are only embedded in steps with >=3 robust positions and sufficient min-entropy, so capacity is entropy-limited and many steps carry no payload. (3) Correctness of a batch requires fewer than half its tokens be tampered. (4) All robustness analysis is token-level; the authors argue higher-level perturbations (tokenization ambiguity, synonym substitution) reduce to token-level operations, but this is a reduction argument rather than direct guarantee. (5) Requires exact synchronization of model, prompt, PRNG seed/key, and sampling parameters between sender and receiver, and depends on a specific DLM (DREAM). Not independently noted by me beyond the paper: comparisons use different base models (DREAM for STEAD vs QWEN2/Qwen/DeepSeek for baselines), which somewhat couples the method with the model choice.

datasets_used: IMDB (movie review sentiment corpus, used for steganalysis pairs)
C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus, used for steganalysis pairs)
1000 covertext/stegotext pairs generated at top-p=0.9 per dataset
quantitative_results: Embedding capacity STEAD (DREAM, top-p=1.0): 84.08 bit/1000 tokens; entropy 7.78 bit/token
Embedding capacity PSARS baseline (QWEN2, top-p=1.0): 13.81 bit/1000 tokens; entropy 3.48 bit/token (~6x lower than STEAD)
STEAD capacity at lower top-p: 58.31 (p=0.98), 36.33 (p=0.92), 33.23 (p=0.90) bit/1000 tokens
Encoding/decoding rate STEAD (p=1.0): 0.9859 / 1.0712 s/bit vs PSARS 1.6583 / 1.5893 s/bit
Steganalysis detection error P_E ~50% -> IMDB: FCN 50.67±0.82%, R-BiLSTM-C 49.00±1.59%, LSTMATT 49.50±1.27%
Robustness under word-level synonym attack at rate 0.1: STEAD >80% extraction correctness while non-robust methods almost entirely fail
Robustness theorem condition: 2(alpha+beta+gamma) < min_s(N_s/L) and beta+gamma < mu/L; neighborhood window mu = max(2, |L - L'|)
Message embedding gated on Nunmask,s >= 3 robust positions and min-entropy >= 1 bit per position
baselines_compared: PSARS (Bai et al. 2025, ARM-based provably robust+secure PSS, on QWEN2) - primary robust baseline
Discop (Ding et al. 2023, distribution-copies PSS, on Qwen and DeepSeek)
SparSamp (Wang et al. 2025, sparse-sampling PSS, on Qwen and DeepSeek)
Baseline DLM without RPE+ECC and NSE (ablation, comparable to ARM robustness)
Steganalyzers used as attackers: FCN, R-BiLSTM-C, LSTMATT
TextAttack framework used for word-level semantic synonym substitution attacks
